title: If _decode throws, finally block calls r.free() on undefined, masking the original error with TypeError
description: The Rust function decode_scaled returns Result<DecodeResult, JsValue>; wasm-bindgen converts Err into a thrown JS exception. If _decode(jpeg, denom) throws, r is undefined. The finally block then executes r.free(), which throws TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'free'), replacing the original meaningful error with an uninformative one. Callers lose the actual decode failure reason.
evidence: The `_decode` call is on line 19, outside the `try` block that starts on line 20. If `_decode` throws (e.g., malformed JPEG causes `read_info()` to error), `r` is never assigned, so `r.free()` in `finally` throws `TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined`, masking the original WASM error. Fix: move `const r = _decode(...)` inside the try block, wrapping with a null-check before `r.free()`.

title: Returned width/height use pre-decode scale() result, not post-decode info(), so dimensions may not match actual decoded pixel buffer
description: out_w and out_h are taken from decoder.scale() at lines 53-54 (the requested target dimensions). After decoder.decode() completes, decoder.info() is called again (line 64) into decoded, but decoded.width/.height are never used for the return value — out_w/out_h are returned instead (lines 71-72). If the jpeg-decoder crate's DCT scaler adjusts dimensions (e.g., rounding to a multiple of 8), the returned width/height could disagree with the actual pixel count in data, leading callers to read out-of-bounds or leave pixels unread.
evidence: In jpeg-decoder 0.3, `Decoder::scale()` returns the actual dimensions the DCT scaler will produce (rounded to block boundaries), which should match the actual output pixel count. The post-decode `decoder.info()` call fetches `pixel_format` for the `to_rgba` conversion but `decoded.width/height` are never used for the return. Whether `scale()` output dimensions always exactly equal actual decoded dimensions requires confirming jpeg-decoder 0.3 internals; if they can diverge by even 1 pixel the to_rgba pixel buffer will have a mismatched length.

title: Cast-before-max pattern: (info.width as u32 / scale).max(1) as u16 silently truncates dimensions > 65535
description: info.width is u16, cast to u32 for division, then .max(1) applied, then cast back to u16. For inputs where info.width > 65535 this truncation would silently corrupt the dimension, though u16 info.width makes this unreachable in practice. More practically the cast chain is fragile: if jpeg-decoder ever widens info.width to u32, the final as u16 becomes a silent truncation bug. The fix is to use u32 throughout and only clamp at the end.
code_excerpt:
let target_w = (info.width as u32 / scale).max(1) as u16;
        let target_h = (info.height as u32 / scale).max(1) as u16;
evidence: `info.width` is `u16` (from jpeg-decoder's `ImageInfo`), so after `as u32 / scale` the result is always ≤ 32767 — the subsequent `as u16` cast can never truncate. The concern about future API widening is speculative and not a present bug; the `scale()` call itself accepts `u16` so the cast is required regardless.

title: No runtime assertion that data.length == width * height * 4 before returning, leaving dimension/buffer mismatches undetected
description: The TypeScript wrapper returns r.data, r.width, r.height without verifying r.data.length === r.width * r.height * 4. If a Rust-side dimension/pixel format bug produces a mismatched buffer, callers (e.g., ImageData constructors) will throw with an opaque size mismatch error rather than a clear assertion at the decode boundary. Adding a single length check here would catch logic-2 class bugs immediately.
evidence: The wrapper has no assertion on `r.data.length === r.width * r.height * 4`. An `ImageData` constructor receiving mismatched dimensions throws a DOM exception with no indication of which JPEG or which dimension is wrong; a guard at the decode boundary would immediately localize such failures. Genuine capability gap with concrete improvement to debuggability.

title: No runtime validation of `denom` allows out-of-contract values to reach WASM
description: The `Denom` type is a TypeScript compile-time union (1|2|4|8) with no runtime enforcement. A JavaScript caller (or any cast via `as any`) can pass 0, 3, 16, or NaN directly into `_decode`. Passing an unexpected denominator to the WASM DCT decoder may trigger undefined behavior, a panic/trap, or a memory-safety violation inside the WASM sandbox. The WASM sandbox limits blast radius but a trap surfaces as an unhandled exception that can crash the Node process.
evidence: The Rust `decode_scaled` function has `match denom { 2 | 4 | 8 => denom as u32, _ => 1 }` — any invalid value (0, 3, NaN coerced to 0 via wasm-bindgen u8) silently falls through to the `_ => 1` arm and produces a full-resolution decode. There is no panic, trap, or undefined behavior; the claim that invalid denom 'may trigger undefined behavior' is incorrect given the explicit wildcard arm.

title: No size or null guard on `jpeg` input before passing to WASM
description: An empty Uint8Array or a very large buffer is passed directly to the WASM decoder without any length check. An empty buffer will likely cause an immediate WASM trap; an extremely large buffer (e.g. >2 GB) may exceed WASM heap limits and produce an opaque OOM trap. Neither case is documented or caught with a useful error message. Adding a minimum-length check (e.g. >= 2 bytes for the JFIF/EXIF SOI marker) and an optional maximum-size guard would surface actionable errors before touching WASM.
code_excerpt:
export function decodeJpegScaled(jpeg: Uint8Array, denom: Denom = 1): JpegDecodeResult {
  const r = _decode(jpeg, denom);
evidence: An empty buffer reaches `decoder.read_info()` which returns `Err(...)` — wasm-bindgen propagates this as a JS exception with a string message, not a silent trap. The claim 'will likely cause an immediate WASM trap' is incorrect; the Rust code uses `?` propagation throughout and `panic = "abort"` means only actual logic panics abort (which read_info does not do on bad input). The error message is admittedly opaque but the behavior is not a trap.

title: No CI dependency-audit step for the compiled WASM binary or its Rust crate supply chain
description: The package ships a pre-built WASM binary from `crates/fast-jpeg/pkg/`. There is no evidence of `cargo audit` or `cargo deny` in CI to flag known CVEs in the libjxl/libjpeg-turbo or image-decoding crates that the fast-jpeg crate may transitively pull in. A dependency-audit job (`cargo audit --deny warnings`) would catch supply-chain vulnerabilities automatically.
evidence: CI configuration is not visible in the reviewed files; `cargo audit` presence or absence cannot be confirmed from this section alone. The concern is legitimate for a shipped WASM binary, but whether it is already handled in CI infrastructure outside this section cannot be determined.

title: WASM exception thrown by _decode escapes before r.free() is called, leaking the WASM object
description: If `_decode(jpeg, denom)` throws (malformed JPEG, allocation failure, WASM trap), the `try` block is never entered so `r` is never assigned and `r.free()` in `finally` cannot run — but more importantly if `_decode` returns successfully yet `r.data`, `r.width`, or `r.height` property access throws (e.g. WASM returns null/undefined), the `finally` still runs fine. The real gap is that a throw inside `_decode` itself means no cleanup is possible because `r` is not in scope. Additionally, any error thrown by `_decode` propagates as an untyped WASM RuntimeError with no context about which JPEG caused it, giving callers no actionable information. Consider wrapping the `_decode` call in the try block and providing a structured error message.
evidence: Duplicate of logic-1: `_decode` is outside the `try` block so a throw leaves `r` undefined and the `finally` block's `r.free()` throws TypeError, masking the original error. This is a real, concrete failure path triggered by any malformed JPEG input. The secondary point about no WASM object leak is also correct — wasm-bindgen throws before returning `r`, so there is nothing to free, but the TypeError masking is the real issue.

title: No guard against _decode returning null/undefined before property access
description: The WASM binding `_decode` is typed as `any`. If the underlying Rust function returns `None` or panics in a way that wasm-bindgen maps to `undefined`, the call `r.data` on line 21 will throw `TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined`, and `r.free()` in `finally` will also throw `TypeError`, masking the original error. A null/undefined check on `r` before destructuring would produce a clear error instead of a confusing secondary exception.
evidence: The Rust function `decode_scaled` is `#[wasm_bindgen]` and returns `Result<DecodeResult, JsValue>`. wasm-bindgen converts `Ok(v)` to a JS object and `Err(e)` to a thrown JS exception — it never returns `undefined` or `null` on the success path. With `panic = "abort"` in the profile, panics terminate the process rather than returning undefined. The described scenario of `_decode` returning `undefined` cannot occur through normal wasm-bindgen code generation.

title: No size guard on jpeg input — arbitrarily large buffers passed directly to WASM
description: The `jpeg` parameter is passed to WASM without any length check. A caller passing a multi-hundred-MB buffer will cause WASM memory allocation to fail or the WASM heap to grow unboundedly. This is especially relevant in a Node environment where the buffer may come from untrusted sources. A simple max-size guard (e.g. 64 MB) would prevent runaway WASM memory growth and provide a clear error to callers.
code_excerpt:
export function decodeJpegScaled(jpeg: Uint8Array, denom: Denom = 1): JpegDecodeResult {
  const r = _decode(jpeg, denom);
evidence: Duplicate of security-2. The jpeg-decoder crate streams the buffer via `Cursor` and does not copy it into WASM heap — the `&[u8]` slice is passed as a view. Allocation failure during pixel decoding returns `Err(...)` through `Result`, not a trap. A max-size guard is a hygiene improvement but the described 'runaway WASM memory growth' mechanism is overstated.

title: No typed error class for JPEG decode failures — callers cannot distinguish decode errors from programming errors
description: All errors from `decodeJpegScaled` propagate as raw WASM RuntimeError or TypeError objects. Callers have no reliable way to distinguish a malformed-JPEG error (recoverable) from a programming error like passing wrong types. A `JpegDecodeError` class wrapping the WASM error with `cause` and a `code` field would let the scheduler or session layer handle decode failures selectively without catching all errors.
evidence: There is no error wrapping in the function; wasm-bindgen errors (which are `JsValue::from_str(...)` strings) propagate as plain JS errors with no `code` or discriminant field. The layer above (decode-handler) catches all errors uniformly; a typed `JpegDecodeError` would let it distinguish malformed-JPEG (skip + continue) from OOM (abort session). Genuine capability gap.

title: r.data returned to caller after r.free() — WASM backing memory freed before caller can copy
description: decodeJpegScaled returns r.data directly and then calls r.free() in the finally block. If the WASM binding allocates the pixel buffer on the WASM heap and r.data is a view into that heap region, calling r.free() before the caller has a chance to copy the data leaves the returned Uint8Array pointing at freed (possibly zeroed or re-used) WASM memory. The fix is to copy the buffer before freeing: `const data = r.data.slice()` — one allocation here prevents every call site from having to defensively copy and eliminates silent corruption for call sites that do not copy.
evidence: The Rust `data()` getter is `pub fn data(&self) -> Vec<u8> { self.data.clone() }` (lib.rs line 27). wasm-bindgen converts the returned `Vec<u8>` into a new `Uint8Array` allocated on the JS heap — not a view into WASM linear memory. The subsequent `r.free()` releases the `DecodeResult` struct's WASM memory, but `r.data` was already copied to JS heap before `free()` executes. There is no use-after-free.

title: r.data Uint8Array is a WASM memory view that becomes invalid after r.free() in finally
description: The WASM object returned by _decode holds a Uint8Array that is a live view into WASM linear memory. The finally block calls r.free(), which releases that memory back to the WASM allocator. The returned JpegDecodeResult.data is therefore a dangling view — any caller that reads data after the function returns may observe zeros or corrupt bytes if the allocator reuses that region. The fix is to copy the data before freeing: `data: r.data.slice()` (creates an owned JS heap copy).
evidence: Identical issue to performance-1. The `data()` getter in lib.rs line 27 does `self.data.clone()`, which wasm-bindgen marshals into a JS-heap-owned `Uint8Array` — not a view into WASM linear memory. `r.free()` cannot invalidate a JS-heap buffer. The finder's model of wasm-bindgen's Vec<u8> marshaling is incorrect.

title: _decode is typed `any` so runtime shape failures silently produce undefined fields in the declared JpegDecodeResult
description: The WASM binding is cast to `any` on line 7, so if the returned object lacks `data`, `width`, or `height` (e.g. on an error path that returns null or a different struct), the destructuring on line 21 silently produces `{ data: undefined, width: undefined, height: undefined }`. The declared return type `JpegDecodeResult` claims all fields are non-optional, creating a false contract. Callers that trust the type and access `result.data.length` will throw at runtime. Adding a minimal runtime shape-check (or a typed wasm-bindgen import) would close this gap.
evidence: wasm-bindgen's error path throws a JS exception rather than returning null or a partial object; the described silent `{ data: undefined }` scenario cannot occur through normal wasm-bindgen code generation. On the success path, `DecodeResult` is a `#[wasm_bindgen]` struct with all three getters defined, so all fields are always present. The `any` cast is a TypeScript hygiene issue but does not produce the described runtime shape failure.

title: Public entry point performs no runtime validation of `jpeg` or `denom`, passing invalid inputs directly to WASM
description: TypeScript's `Denom` union and `Uint8Array` types are erased at runtime. A JavaScript caller can pass `denom = 3` or an empty `Uint8Array`, which reach the WASM `decode_scaled` function unchecked. Depending on the Rust implementation, this may produce a WASM panic (opaque RuntimeError), undefined behavior, or a silent wrong result. The public API contract should validate that `denom` is in {1, 2, 4, 8} and that `jpeg.length > 0` before crossing the FFI boundary, throwing a typed Error with a clear message.
code_excerpt:
export function decodeJpegScaled(jpeg: Uint8Array, denom: Denom = 1): JpegDecodeResult {
  const r = _decode(jpeg, denom);
evidence: Invalid `denom` is safe: the Rust `_ => 1` wildcard arm silently clamps to full-resolution decode without panic. Empty `jpeg` results in a propagated `Err(...)` as a JS exception, not a trap or undefined behavior. The description's claim of 'WASM panic, undefined behavior, or silent wrong result' is only partially correct (silent wrong result for bad denom is true — but it produces a valid full-resolution image, not corruption). Adding guards for better error messages is reasonable hygiene but this is not a safety issue.

title: Errors from WASM decode propagate as opaque RuntimeError with no typed error contract for callers
description: If `_decode` throws (corrupt JPEG, allocation failure, WASM trap), the exception escapes `decodeJpegScaled` unmodified. Callers receive an opaque WebAssembly.RuntimeError or a wasm-bindgen JsValue error with no predictable shape. There is no documented error contract and no wrapping into a domain-specific error class. This makes it impossible for callers to distinguish recoverable errors (bad input) from fatal ones (OOM) without inspecting the raw exception message string.
evidence: Duplicate of errors-4 with additional contract framing. The Rust error path uses `JsValue::from_str(&e.to_string())` which produces a plain JS Error with only a message string. No error code, no class hierarchy, no way for callers to catch only `JpegDecodeError` vs other errors. Real gap: if the decode-handler above catches errors from this function it cannot route decode failures vs programming errors without string matching.

title: No tests verify the shape and pixel correctness of the public API contract
description: The module exposes a single public function whose WASM binding is loaded at require-time with no test coverage visible in this file or adjacent test files. A contract test suite would: (1) confirm JpegDecodeResult fields are always defined non-null, (2) verify width*height*4 == data.length for valid inputs, (3) verify a typed Error is thrown for invalid denom/empty buffer, and (4) confirm the returned data buffer is not a dangling WASM view. These tests would catch the use-after-free issue (contracts-1) and shape drift (contracts-2) at CI time.
evidence: No test file found adjacent to `packages/fast-jpeg/src/index.ts`; the module is a single 25-line file with no test coverage. The public API contract (field presence, buffer length, error behavior) is entirely untested. This is a genuine gap — the logic-1 (error masking) bug would be caught by a test that verifies malformed JPEG produces the original WASM error, not a TypeError.
